caveats: a: The occupational source is NAICS 711200, not 711212, and includes sports teams and clubs whose athlete and media wage mix is unlike a racetrack.
a: O*NET task lists establish task content but do not measure current automation, time shares, wage weights, or technical substitutability.
a: The estimate excludes already automated self-service and online wagering from the remaining opportunity, but no track-level baseline was obtainable.
rho: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured five-year adoption rate.
rho: Capital constraints at small tracks, legacy totalizator integrations, union arrangements, and state-by-state approvals could slow deployment.
rho: HISA governs Thoroughbred racing nationally but does not cover every horse-racing breed or every included workflow.
e: The frozen firm-count input is for all of 711212, while this lens excludes auto and dog tracks, making the population mismatch material.
e: Establishment classification does not reveal firm-level control, contract structure, nonprofit status, or whether racetrack operations are economically separable from a casino.
e: The default lens is difficult to apply because many operators sell experiences directly to patrons rather than a conventional outsourced B2B service.
s5: BizBuySell is a voluntary broad small-business marketplace and does not measure racetrack transactions or a transfer probability.
s5: No defensible national data on horse-track owner age, succession plans, or qualifying control transfers was found.
s5: A transfer of real estate, racing dates, or a wagering license may not constitute transfer of an intact operating firm.
q: California is one large state and not representative of every jurisdiction or breed.
q: The source describes distribution of wagering revenue, not direct contractual sharing of AI savings.
q: The value-capture mix differs for admissions, hospitality, sponsorship, media, concessions, and wagering.
d5: The source covers Thoroughbred racing in the U.S., Canada, and Puerto Rico rather than only U.S. establishments.
d5: Handle is nominal and is not itself constant-price demand; race count omits quality, attendance, digital engagement, and non-racing venue use.
d5: Historical contraction does not identify the future effects of state subsidies, new gaming formats, media distribution, or track redevelopment.
o: HISA applies to Thoroughbred racing and is not a direct quantity forecast.
o: Some customer-facing wagering and information services can migrate entirely to apps or third-party platforms even while a track operator remains required.
o: The estimate concerns operator-required service quantity, not preservation of current headcount.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring event and wagering revenue from pari-mutuel track commissions, admissions, hospitality, concessions, sponsorship, media or simulcast arrangements, and facility use. Customers include repeat bettors and spectators as well as horsemen, sponsors, and event counterparties; wagering economics are heavily prescribed by state law and horsemen agreements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes horse, auto, and dog racetrack models with materially different regulation, workflows, and revenue mechanisms. The lens is narrowed to horse-racetrack operators for a coherent operating case, without selecting operators for attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Auto and dog racetracks; owners, trainers, jockeys, and other racing participants classified in 711219; pure casinos, online wagering platforms without track operations, captive internal units, public or nonprofit facilities without transferable control, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Privately controlled U.S. horse-racetrack operators that repeatedly stage live racing, run the racing office and wagering operation, maintain the racing surface and facility, and serve bettors, spectators, horsemen, sponsors, and event users.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader four-digit Spectator Sports occupation mix rather than six-digit racetracks, and O*NET describes tasks rather than measuring AI substitution. I mapped administrative support, sales, marketing, customer-information, bookkeeping, scheduling, wagering-record, and content tasks to partial exposure, while assigning little exposure to security, food service, grounds, maintenance, veterinary, safety, and live-event physical work. The broader industry includes highly paid athletes, coaches, and media staff that are not track employees, so its wage weighting can bias exposure upward; omission of racetrack-specific wagering clerks can bias it downward.
caveats: The occupational source is NAICS 711200, not 711212, and includes sports teams and clubs whose athlete and media wage mix is unlike a racetrack.
O*NET task lists establish task content but do not measure current automation, time shares, wage weights, or technical substitutability.
The estimate excludes already automated self-service and online wagering from the remaining opportunity, but no track-level baseline was obtainable.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit employer payroll extract by SOC for 711212, paired with time-and-motion interviews of racing-office, mutuel, marketing, finance, compliance, and facility teams at at least ten horse tracks and a current baseline of deployed automation by task, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The BLS proxy reports management at 9.60% of employment, sales at 8.13%, and office and administrative support at 8.17% in Spectator Sports, alongside substantial physical and live-event work. O*NET shows that gambling writers and runners receive, verify, record, and settle wagers, while amusement attendants combine ticketing and information with safety monitoring, patron direction, cleaning, and inspection. AI can absorb portions of records, reconciliation, scheduling, customer response, marketing, reporting, and content workflows, but core race-day, safety, surface, facility, veterinary, hospitality, and crowd tasks remain physical or accountable-human work.

d5.bridge: The Jockey Club series measures historical North American Thoroughbred race count and nominal pari-mutuel handle, rather than a forward U.S. constant-price, constant-quality quantity index for all included track services. I treat race count as the principal physical-volume indicator and nominal handle as a demand cross-check, then extrapolate a moderated five-year decline from the observed 2019-to-2025 contraction. Inclusion of Canada and Puerto Rico and exclusion of non-Thoroughbred racing can bias either way; using race count may understate digital wagering engagement and overstate the importance of live dates.
caveats: The source covers Thoroughbred racing in the U.S., Canada, and Puerto Rico rather than only U.S. establishments.
Handle is nominal and is not itself constant-price demand; race count omits quality, attendance, digital engagement, and non-racing venue use.
Historical contraction does not identify the future effects of state subsidies, new gaming formats, media distribution, or track redevelopment.
d5.change_evidence: A U.S.-only monthly panel through 2026 combining live race days, paid attendance, unique bettors, inflation-adjusted handle, admissions, sponsorship, hospitality, simulcast rights, and non-race event use by track would materially change the forecast.
d5.rationale: The Jockey Club reports 40,798 races in 2019 and 33,410 in 2025, while total handle was $11.722 billion in 2019 and $11.814 billion in 2025 in nominal dollars. The physical racing calendar thus contracted materially even as nominal wagering was roughly flat. A continuing but slower decline is the central case; stable or improving wagering engagement and venue diversification support the high case, while further track closures, aging audiences, animal-welfare pressure, and substitute gambling support the low case.

caveats: The frozen firm-count input is for all of 711212, while this lens excludes auto and dog tracks, making the population mismatch material.
Establishment classification does not reveal firm-level control, contract structure, nonprofit status, or whether racetrack operations are economically separable from a casino.
The default lens is difficult to apply because many operators sell experiences directly to patrons rather than a conventional outsourced B2B service.
e.change_evidence: A verified census of all 711212 firms cross-walked to track type, ultimate owner, tax status, public control, casino affiliation, revenue mix, EBITDA band, and willingness or legal ability to transfer control would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Census defines 711212 to include operators of auto, dog, and horse racetracks, while the coherent lens retains only privately controlled horse-track operators with repeat external revenue and transferable operations. Many facilities are tied to governments, fairs, member organizations, casinos, or larger strategic groups, and participant-side businesses belong in 711219 rather than this code. With no six-digit ownership table by track type, the eligible share is necessarily a bounded estimate.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 711212 ($1,249,453,280) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 711212 ($8,703,921,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1664 (margins.json[71]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Recreation EBITDA/Sales 16.64%); revenue/employee $273,107 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 711212); class <5: 300 firms, avg revenue/firm $471,467 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $78,452 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,431,849 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $238,260 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,092,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $680,909 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,388,977 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,229,526 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,226,406 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,192,874 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $265,400,520 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $44,162,647 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 76

o.bridge: HISA requirements establish accountable on-track roles, maintenance, testing, reporting, inspection, and accreditation for Thoroughbred tracks, not the exact share of future service quantity requiring an operator. I map the persistence of those facility, safety, regulatory, wagering, and live-event obligations to operator-required quantity, while allowing self-service wagering and software-mediated customer activity to remove some operator labor without eliminating the licensed operating entity. Because HISA does not cover every breed, the proxy may overstate uniformity but the physical-facility requirement remains directionally relevant.
caveats: HISA applies to Thoroughbred racing and is not a direct quantity forecast.
Some customer-facing wagering and information services can migrate entirely to apps or third-party platforms even while a track operator remains required.
The estimate concerns operator-required service quantity, not preservation of current headcount.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory approvals for remotely operated or operator-light race meets, track staffing benchmarks before and after digital wagering adoption, and audited service maps distinguishing eliminated, self-served, vendor-supplied, and legally accountable activities would materially change this share.
o.rationale: A live horse race requires a maintained and tested surface, facility readiness, racing-office controls, safety and medical roles, wagering integrity, crowd management, and an accountable regulated operator. HISA says track operations span management, the racing office, maintenance and facilities, and accreditation; it also requires written maintenance procedures, pre-meet and daily surface tests, documentation, onsite inspections, and annual reports. Online betting and self-service can reduce front-counter activity, but they do not replace the entity responsible for staging and governing the race meet.

q.bridge: The California Horse Racing Board measures statutory wagering takeout and its prescribed distribution, not retention of AI-created gross benefit. I use that structure to infer low contractual pass-through of incremental back-office savings but meaningful leakage through regulated allocations, horsemen relationships, promotional rebates, customer acquisition, and competitive reinvestment. California may overstate legal rigidity relative to other states, pushing the inferred retention share downward; fixed-price admissions, sponsorship, and hospitality can push it upward.
caveats: California is one large state and not representative of every jurisdiction or breed.
The source describes distribution of wagering revenue, not direct contractual sharing of AI savings.
The value-capture mix differs for admissions, hospitality, sponsorship, media, concessions, and wagering.
q.change_evidence: Track-level contracts and five-year cohorts linking realized operating savings to takeout changes, rebates, purses, sponsor renewals, admissions, concessions pricing, handle, and EBITDA would materially change retention; state-by-state takeout and distribution rules are also needed.
q.rationale: Horse tracks do not generally bill customers on cost-plus labor contracts, so an internal labor saving is not automatically passed through. However, the core wagering revenue pool is constrained: California states that takeout is set by law, lists conventional Thoroughbred takeout at 15.43%, and says remaining funds after mandated deductions are divided in a prescribed manner between purses and track commissions. Savings in finance, marketing, service, and compliance can therefore remain with the operator, but competition for handle, statutory economics, horsemen negotiations, and the need to improve the fan product prevent full retention over five years.

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured five-year adoption rate.
Capital constraints at small tracks, legacy totalizator integrations, union arrangements, and state-by-state approvals could slow deployment.
HISA governs Thoroughbred racing nationally but does not cover every horse-racing breed or every included workflow.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation logs from track-management, totalizator, CRM, finance, and HISA-reporting vendors showing task-level adoption, exception rates, labor hours removed, and audit findings across two full race meets would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Within the exposed task set, standardized marketing, customer-response, finance, reconciliation, scheduling, records, and first-draft compliance work can be implemented through existing systems and human review. Implementation is slower in wagering settlement, regulatory status enforcement, safety reporting, and race operations because errors are auditable and consequential. HISA requires daily surface testing and documentation, written procedures, annual reports, onsite inspections, and named accountable roles, favoring assistive implementation over unattended automation.

caveats: BizBuySell is a voluntary broad small-business marketplace and does not measure racetrack transactions or a transfer probability.
No defensible national data on horse-track owner age, succession plans, or qualifying control transfers was found.
A transfer of real estate, racing dates, or a wagering license may not constitute transfer of an intact operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: Secretary-of-state ownership histories, state racing-license change-of-control dockets, property records, PitchBook or Capital IQ transactions, and confidential interviews with track owners and specialist gaming brokers covering the last ten years would materially change the five-year probability.
s5.rationale: Eligible tracks are scarce, regulated, location-specific, and often asset-heavy, so a control transfer is less routine than a main-street service-business sale. The broad small-business marketplace nevertheless reported 9,546 closed transactions in 2024, evidence that a functioning transfer channel exists but not a denominator-based racetrack probability. The base allows for owner succession and strategic sales while discounting internal reorganizations, facility closures, license-only transactions, and transfers where operations cannot be separated from real estate or gaming rights.

principal_driver: Fixed-price and statutory revenue structures let a capable operator retain part of repeatable administrative and commercial workflow savings without removing the accountable track operation.
principal_weakness: A contracting live-racing calendar combines with an exceptionally thin and unmeasured pool of eligible, transferable independent horse-track operators.
